description: When no dhcp-relay l2vpn-clients is configured and the client MAC is not learned then the first L2VPN DHCP client packet is dropped.

messages.content: For 8325 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what caveat is documented for DHCP Relay?
Feature Caveat: DHCP Relay - When no dhcp-relay l2vpn-clients is configured and the client MAC is not learned then the first L2VPN DHCP client packet is dropped.

description: On 8325, 8325P and 8325H Switch series, iBGP split-horizon rule does not apply across address families For example, routes exchanged between VSX peers over iBGP may be re-advertised into other families, which can introduce routing loops. To prevent this behavior, route-maps (for example, using BGP communities) should be used to classify the routes and explicitly control their propagation. In particular, it is recommended to enforce policies using route-maps that restrict the advertisement of these routes into the EVPN address family.

messages.content: For 8325 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what caveat is documented for BGP?

Feature Caveat: BGP - On 8325, 8325P and 8325H Switch series, iBGP split-horizon rule does not apply across address families. For example, routes exchanged between VSX peers over iBGP may be re-advertised into other families, which can introduce routing loops. To prevent this behavior, route-maps (for example, using BGP communities) should be used to classify the routes and explicitly control their propagation. In particular, it is recommended to enforce policies using route-maps that restrict the advertisement of these routes into the EVPN address family.

description: When configuring the same BGP neighbor under both IPv4 and IPv6 address families on a router, if a maximum prefix limit is set on one address family (like IPv4), exceeding that limit can potentially impact the BGP session for the other address family (IPv6) as well.

messages.content: For 8325 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what limitation is mentioned for BGP?

Feature Caveat: BGP - When configuring the same BGP neighbor under both IPv4 and IPv6 address families on a router, if a maximum prefix limit is set on one address family (like IPv4), exceeding that limit can potentially impact the BGP session for the other address family (IPv6) as well.

description: If there is no user configuration for DNS servers and HPE Aruba Networking Central is configured, the switch defaults to using Google's DNS server at 8.8.8.8. HPE Aruba Networking Central is enabled by default across all switch platforms.

messages.content: For 8325 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what caveat is documented for Activate?

Feature Caveat: Activate - If there is no user configuration for DNS servers and HPE Aruba Networking Central is configured, the switch defaults to using Google's DNS server at 8.8.8.8. HPE Aruba Networking Central is enabled by default across all switch platforms.

description: Duplicate Address Detection (DAD) status indicated a duplicate for IPv6 anycast gateway configuration. As a workaround, configure an active gateway IPv6 address and SVI IPv6 address, as this can prevent DAD issues. Issuing the commands interface shut followed by interface no-shut can help in resolving the issue.

messages.content: For 8325 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what ARP caveat is documented?

Feature Caveat: ARP - Duplicate Address Detection (DAD) status indicated a duplicate for IPv6 anycast gateway configuration. As a workaround, configure an active gateway IPv6 address and SVI IPv6 address, as this can prevent DAD issues. Issuing the commands interface shut followed by interface no-shut can help in resolving the issue.

description: After an issue with duplicate IPs on two VXLAN tunnel endpoints (VTEPs) is resolved, the kernel will have the ARP entry, but the output of the show arp and show evpn mac-ip commands do not show that IP. As a workaround, flap the physical interface.

messages.content: For 8325 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what limitation is mentioned for ARP?

Feature Caveat: ARP - After an issue with duplicate IPs on two VXLAN tunnel endpoints (VTEPs) is resolved, the kernel will have the ARP entry, but the output of the show arp and show evpn mac-ip commands do not show that IP. As a workaround, flap the physical interface.

description: The next-hop-unchanged option needs to be explicitly configured to preserve nexthop while advertising routes to eBGP peers, in the L2VPN EVPN address-family. For example: Copy router bgp

messages.content: For 8325 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what additional caveat is documented for BGP?

Feature Caveat: BGP - The next-hop-unchanged option needs to be explicitly configured to preserve the nexthop while advertising routes to eBGP peers in the L2VPN EVPN address-family. For example: Copy router bgp.

description: Symptom: Dual-port AP uplinks connected via a LAG intermittently show member interfaces as “Blocked by Port Access Security.” the LAG stops forwarding and the end device cannot onboard until the interfaces are toggled. Scenario: This issue may occur when port-access device-profile is enabled on the LAG with mode block-until-profile-applied, using LLDP for profiling. When the LAG member that had the LLDP neighbor goes Layer-1 down, subsequent authentication fails and the interface becomes blocked.

description: Symptom: Policy hitcounts commands using the vrf specifier do not include policies applied to L3 VNIs associated with the specified VRF. Scenario: This issue may occur when configuring an L3 VNI with a VRF, applying a policy to it, and sending traffic to increment hitcounts. Attempting to show policy hitcounts for the VRF fails and a report that no policy is applied to the VRF. Attempting to clear hitcounts for the VRF has no effect. Workaround: When showing or clearing hitcounts for all applications associated with the VRF, use a separate command to show or clear hitcounts for each L3 VNI.

description: Symptom: DHCP clients connected to a switch configured as a DHCP relay agent may fail to obtain an IP address when DHCPv4 Snooping and DHCPv4 Relay are both enabled on the same VLAN. DHCP clients that perform explicit source MAC address validation may drop the relayed DHCP response packets. Scenario: This issue occurs when both DHCPv4 Relay and DHCPv4 Snooping are enabled on the same VLAN. In this configuration, DHCP server responses (Offer/Ack) are forwarded to the client with an invalid (all-zeros) source MAC address in the Ethernet header. Workaround: Avoid enabling DHCPv4 Snooping and DHCPv4 Relay simultaneously on the same VLAN.

description: Symptom: EST-based certificate auto-enrollment and re-enrollment fails with an SSL connect error (CURL error 35) on AOS-CX 10.16 and later when the EST server requires TLS client certificate authentication and enforces it via TLS renegotiation. Scenario: This issue may occur when the EST client first retrieves CA certificates over an unauthenticated TLS connection and caches the session. When the server subsequently requests a client certificate by initiating TLS renegotiation on the reused session, the switch rejects the renegotiation, causing enrollment to fail. This behavior is observed with EST servers such as EJBCA when configured to require mutual TLS (mTLS) client certificate authentication. Workaround: Configure the EST server to accept password-based authentication (HTTP Digest or Basic) as an alternative to requiring TLS client certificate authentication on the enrollment endpoint, and configure username and password credentials in the AOS-CX EST profile. This changes the authentication method for EST enrollment.

description: Symptom: When ip forward-protocol udp is configured with a non-classful subnet broadcast address as the destination (for example, 10.232.104.127 for a /25 subnet), the forwarded packet is sent as unicast to that address instead of being broadcast on the destination subnet. Hosts on the destination subnet do not receive the forwarded UDP broadcast. Scenario: This issue occurs when the destination address configured in ip forward-protocol udp is a non-classful subnet broadcast address (not ending in.255). The UDP broadcast forwarder only recognizes classful subnet broadcast addresses (ending in.255) as broadcast destinations. Any other address, including valid subnet broadcast addresses for prefix lengths shorter than /24 (such as /25, /26, etc.), is treated as a unicast destination. The switch identifies a configured address as a broadcast destination solely based on whether the last octet is 255, regardless of the subnet prefix length. Workaround: Use only classful subnet broadcast addresses (ending in.255) as destinations in ip forward-protocol udp when broadcast delivery on the destination subnet is required. For non-/24 subnets where the broadcast address does not end in.255, configure each unicast destination host IP individually. A maximum of 8 forward-protocol entries are supported per interface.

description: Symptom: BGP peer group member sessions do not reset when the include-TCP-options setting is modified, while individual peer sessions reset as expected. This creates inconsistent behavior between peer group and standalone peer configurations. Scenario: This issue occurs when modifying or removing the include-TCP-options setting on a BGP peer group that has TCP Authentication Option (TCP-AO) configured. Individual peer sessions respond correctly to the configuration change by resetting, but peer group member sessions remain established without resetting.

description: Symptom: BGP session with TCP Authentication Option (TCP-AO) remains in Established state even when a keychain key mismatch exists between peers. The session does not reset when the matching key is deleted from one peer, and manual session reset using clear bgp \* does not resolve the issue. This creates a security vulnerability as the session continues operating with mismatched authentication parameters. Scenario: This issue occurs when a matching keychain key is deleted from one BGP peer after both peers have been configured with the same key. The deletion creates a parameter mismatch between the two peers (different send-id, recv-id, or key string values), but the BGP session fails to detect this mismatch and remains established instead of resetting.

However, after confirming the removal by entering yes, the keychain is not actually deleted and remains in the configuration. Scenario: This issue occurs when trying to remove a keychain that is actively associated with a BGP neighbor configured with TCP Authentication Option (TCP-AO). description: Symptom: When BGP graceful restart is triggered on a TCP-AO authenticated BGP peer, the peer router does not mark the received routes as stale (indicated by the S prefix). Instead, routes are immediately removed from the routing table, causing a temporary loss of traffic visibility and convergence delay until the restarting peer comes back online. Scenario: This issue occurs when performing a graceful restart (redundancy switchover or hpe-routing restart) on a BGP peer that has TCP Authentication Option (TCP-AO) configured with keychain authentication. The peer router fails to enter graceful restart helper mode and does not preserve routes with stale markers during the restart window.

Scenario: This issue occurs when the include-TCP-options setting is removed from a BGP peer group that has TCP Authentication Option (TCP-AO) configured with a keychain. While one side removes the parameter, the peer group member sessions on both sides continue to remain established instead of tearing down due to the configuration mismatch.

description: Symptom: ISL connectivity may be affected as the full MTU override has not been applied to some ports. VSX connectivity between peers may be affected. Scenario: This issue may occur during bring up of ISL ports and may result in MTU override not being set correctly during port configuration. Ports that were missed during this configuration remain missed, even if more ports are added to ISL later. Workaround: Fully unconfigure the ISL and affected ports then reconfigure for proper MTU override to be applied.

description: Symptom: DHCP clients connected via a relay agent either fail to obtain an IP address or receive an IP address from an incorrect DHCP pool instead of the pool intended for their subnet. Scenario: This issue occurs when the DHCP server has multiple pools configured with IP ranges that span non-default subnet boundaries (e.g., /23), and the prefix-len parameter is not explicitly specified in the range command. When a relay agent forwards client DISCOVER packets, the DHCP server uses the relay agent's gateway IP address (giaddr) to identify the matching pool. Without prefix-len, the server cannot determine the pool's subnet boundary and fails to correctly map the giaddr to the intended pool. Workaround: Explicitly specify prefix-len in the pool range configuration to match the intended subnet mask. For example: dhcp-server vrf default pool pool_name_ABC range 172.22.0.4 172.22.1.254 prefix-len <netmask>.

Scenario: This issue occurs when the DHCP server has multiple pools configured with IP ranges that span non-default subnet boundaries (e.g., /23), and the prefix-len parameter is not explicitly specified in the range command. When a relay agent forwards client DISCOVER packets, the DHCP server uses the relay agent's gateway IP address (giaddr) to identify the matching pool. Without prefix-len, the server cannot determine the pool's subnet boundary and fails to correctly map the giaddr to the intended pool. Workaround: Explicitly specify prefix-len in the pool range configuration to match the intended subnet mask. For example: dhcp-server vrf default pool pool_name_ABC range 172.22.0.4 172.22.1.254 prefix-len <netmask>.

description: Symptom: Routes take up to two minutes to switch to an alternate path when an active BGP neighbor is disabled, leading to delays in traffic rerouting. Scenario: This issue occurs in a network utilizing BGP routing. When a BGP session is disabled on one switch, the corresponding routes remain in a stale state until the stale path timer expires, causing traffic loss during the transition.

description: Symptom: When an ACL that has logging in any of its entries gets applied to a VRF in the control plane, rebooting in this state causes the switch to crash and infinitely reboot until the log portion of the ACL configuration is removed. Scenario: This issue may occur on any ACL with an entry that has a logging keyword that is applied to a control-plane ACL. Workaround: Removing the log action from any ACL entry or unapplying any ACL with a log entry from a VRF addresses this issue. For each VRF the routes are leaked into, the neighbor insert is sent once to the stack. The log entry can either be put back or re-applied after the switch has booted up to retain configuration.

description: Symptom: Dual-port AP uplinks connected via a LAG intermittently show member interfaces as “Blocked by Port Access Security.” the LAG stops forwarding and the end device cannot onboard until the interfaces are toggled. Scenario: This issue may occur when port-access device-profile is enabled on the LAG with mode block-until-profile-applied, using LLDP for profiling. When the LAG member that had the LLDP neighbor goes Layer-1 down, subsequent authentication fails and the interface becomes blocked.

description: Symptom: Policy hitcounts commands using the vrf specifier do not include policies applied to L3 VNIs associated with the specified VRF. Scenario: This issue may occur when configuring an L3 VNI with a VRF, applying a policy to it, and sending traffic to increment hitcounts. Attempting to show policy hitcounts for the VRF fails and a report that no policy is applied to the VRF. Attempting to clear hitcounts for the VRF has no effect. Workaround: When showing or clearing hitcounts for all applications associated with the VRF, use a separate command to show or clear hitcounts for each L3 VNI.

description: Symptom: DHCP clients connected to a switch configured as a DHCP relay agent may fail to obtain an IP address when DHCPv4 Snooping and DHCPv4 Relay are both enabled on the same VLAN. DHCP clients that perform explicit source MAC address validation may drop the relayed DHCP response packets. Scenario: This issue occurs when both DHCPv4 Relay and DHCPv4 Snooping are enabled on the same VLAN. In this configuration, DHCP server responses (Offer/Ack) are forwarded to the client with an invalid (all-zeros) source MAC address in the Ethernet header. Workaround: Avoid enabling DHCPv4 Snooping and DHCPv4 Relay simultaneously on the same VLAN.

description: Symptom: EST-based certificate auto-enrollment and re-enrollment fails with an SSL connect error (CURL error 35) on AOS-CX 10.16 and later when the EST server requires TLS client certificate authentication and enforces it via TLS renegotiation. Scenario: This issue may occur when the EST client first retrieves CA certificates over an unauthenticated TLS connection and caches the session. When the server subsequently requests a client certificate by initiating TLS renegotiation on the reused session, the switch rejects the renegotiation, causing enrollment to fail. This behavior is observed with EST servers such as EJBCA when configured to require mutual TLS (mTLS) client certificate authentication. Workaround: Configure the EST server to accept password-based authentication (HTTP Digest or Basic) as an alternative to requiring TLS client certificate authentication on the enrollment endpoint, and configure username and password credentials in the AOS-CX EST profile. This changes the authentication method for EST enrollment.

Scenario: This issue may occur when the EST client first retrieves CA certificates over an unauthenticated TLS connection and caches the session. When the server subsequently requests a client certificate by initiating TLS renegotiation on the reused session, the switch rejects the renegotiation, causing enrollment to fail. This behavior is observed with EST servers such as EJBCA when configured to require mutual TLS (mTLS) client certificate authentication. Workaround: Configure the EST server to accept password-based authentication (HTTP Digest or Basic) as an alternative to requiring TLS client certificate authentication on the enrollment endpoint, and configure username and password credentials in the AOS-CX EST profile. This changes the authentication method for EST enrollment.

description: Symptom: When ip forward-protocol udp is configured with a non-classful subnet broadcast address as the destination (for example, 10.232.104.127 for a /25 subnet), the forwarded packet is sent as unicast to that address instead of being broadcast on the destination subnet. Hosts on the destination subnet do not receive the forwarded UDP broadcast. Scenario: This issue occurs when the destination address configured in ip forward-protocol udp is a non-classful subnet broadcast address (not ending in.255). The UDP broadcast forwarder only recognizes classful subnet broadcast addresses (ending in.255) as broadcast destinations. Any other address, including valid subnet broadcast addresses for prefix lengths shorter than /24 (such as /25, /26, etc.), is treated as a unicast destination. The switch identifies a configured address as a broadcast destination solely based on whether the last octet is 255, regardless of the subnet prefix length. Workaround: Use only classful subnet broadcast addresses (ending in.255) as destinations in ip forward-protocol udp when broadcast delivery on the destination subnet is required. For non-/24 subnets where the broadcast address does not end in.255, configure each unicast destination host IP individually. A maximum of 8 forward-protocol entries are supported per interface.

description: Symptom: BGP peer group member sessions do not reset when the include-TCP-options setting is modified, while individual peer sessions reset as expected. This creates inconsistent behavior between peer group and standalone peer configurations. Scenario: This issue occurs when modifying or removing the include-TCP-options setting on a BGP peer group that has TCP Authentication Option (TCP-AO) configured. Individual peer sessions respond correctly to the configuration change by resetting, but peer group member sessions remain established without resetting.

description: Symptom: BGP session with TCP Authentication Option (TCP-AO) remains in Established state even when a keychain key mismatch exists between peers. The session does not reset when the matching key is deleted from one peer, and manual session reset using clear bgp \* does not resolve the issue. This creates a security vulnerability as the session continues operating with mismatched authentication parameters. Scenario: This issue occurs when a matching keychain key is deleted from one BGP peer after both peers have been configured with the same key. The deletion creates a parameter mismatch between the two peers (different send-id, recv-id, or key string values), but the BGP session fails to detect this mismatch and remains established instead of resetting.

description: Symptom: BGP session with TCP Authentication Option (TCP-AO) remains in Established state even when a keychain key mismatch exists between peers. The session does not reset when the matching key is deleted from one peer, and manual session reset using clear bgp \* does not resolve the issue. This creates a security vulnerability as the session continues operating with mismatched authentication parameters. Scenario: This issue occurs when a matching keychain key is deleted from one BGP peer after both peers have been configured with the same key. The deletion creates a parameter mismatch between the two peers (different send-id, recv-id, or key string values), but the BGP session fails to detect this mismatch and remains established instead of resetting.

Scenario: This issue occurs when trying to remove a keychain that is actively associated with a BGP neighbor configured with TCP Authentication Option (TCP-AO). description: Symptom: When BGP graceful restart is triggered on a TCP-AO authenticated BGP peer, the peer router does not mark the received routes as stale (indicated by the S prefix). Instead, routes are immediately removed from the routing table, causing a temporary loss of traffic visibility and convergence delay until the restarting peer comes back online. Scenario: This issue occurs when performing a graceful restart (redundancy switchover or hpe-routing restart) on a BGP peer that has TCP Authentication Option (TCP-AO) configured with keychain authentication. The peer router fails to enter graceful restart helper mode and does not preserve routes with stale markers during the restart window.

description: Symptom: BGP peer group sessions with TCP-AO configured remain in Established state even after the include-TCP-options parameter is removed on one side. The sessions do not reset as expected, and they remain up even after manual session reset using clear bgp \*. Scenario: This issue occurs when the include-TCP-options setting is removed from a BGP peer group that has TCP Authentication Option (TCP-AO) configured with a keychain. While one side removes the parameter, the peer group member sessions on both sides continue to remain established instead of tearing down due to the configuration mismatch.

description: Symptom: ISL connectivity may be affected as the full MTU override has not been applied to some ports. VSX connectivity between peers may be affected. Scenario: This issue may occur during bring up of ISL ports and may result in MTU override not being set correctly during port configuration. Ports that were missed during this configuration remain missed, even if more ports are added to ISL later. Workaround: Fully unconfigure the ISL and affected ports then reconfigure for proper MTU override to be applied.

description: Symptom: DHCP clients connected via a relay agent either fail to obtain an IP address or receive an IP address from an incorrect DHCP pool instead of the pool intended for their subnet. Scenario: This issue occurs when the DHCP server has multiple pools configured with IP ranges that span non-default subnet boundaries (e.g., /23), and the prefix-len parameter is not explicitly specified in the range command. When a relay agent forwards client DISCOVER packets, the DHCP server uses the relay agent's gateway IP address (giaddr) to identify the matching pool. Without prefix-len, the server cannot determine the pool's subnet boundary and fails to correctly map the giaddr to the intended pool. Workaround: Explicitly specify prefix-len in the pool range configuration to match the intended subnet mask. For example: dhcp-server vrf default pool pool_name_ABC range 172.22.0.4 172.22.1.254 prefix-len <netmask>.

description: Symptom: Routes take up to two minutes to switch to an alternate path when an active BGP neighbor is disabled, leading to delays in traffic rerouting. Scenario: This issue occurs in a network utilizing BGP routing. When a BGP session is disabled on one switch, the corresponding routes remain in a stale state until the stale path timer expires, causing traffic loss during the transition.

description: Symptom: When an ACL that has logging in any of its entries gets applied to a VRF in the control plane, rebooting in this state causes the switch to crash and infinitely reboot until the log portion of the ACL configuration is removed. Scenario: This issue may occur on any ACL with an entry that has a logging keyword that is applied to a control-plane ACL. Workaround: Removing the log action from any ACL entry or unapplying any ACL with a log entry from a VRF addresses this issue. For each VRF the routes are leaked into, the neighbor insert is sent once to the stack. The log entry can either be put back or re-applied after the switch has booted up to retain configuration.
